target.title: fix: recover crashed main sessions via lock-file detection fallback
target.body:
## Summary

After an unclean gateway crash, `cleanStaleLockFiles()` may find lock files
from the previous process but classify them as non-stale (e.g. PID was
reused, or staleness check passes). When `cleaned.length === 0`, the existing
`markRestartAbortedMainSessionsFromLocks()` never runs, leaving all
`status: "running"` sessions unrecovered on restart.

This adds `markCrashedMainSessionsFromRemainingLocks()` which runs in the
session-locks sidecar at startup (setImmediate, before any client can
connect). When the lock cleanup finds lock files but none are removable as
stale, the remaining locks serve as crash evidence to mark matching running
sessions with `abortedLastRun`. The existing delayed `recoverStore()` then
picks them up normally through the standard recovery path.

## What changed

- Added `markCrashedMainSessionsFromRemainingLocks()` — marks running sessions
  whose lock paths match remaining non-stale locks inspected at startup
- Wired into `server-startup-post-attach.ts` session-locks sidecar: when
  `cleaned.length === 0` but `locks.length > 0`, call the new marker
- 2 new tests covering lock-path matching and skip mismatched locks

## Safety

- Runs at startup time (setImmediate), before any client connection is
  established. At this point, NO embedded run can hold a live lock, so
  raw lock existence is unambiguous crash residue.
- Only activates when `cleaned.length === 0` — the normal stale-lock
  recovery path takes precedence.
- Sessions without a matching lock are skipped; idle sessions are never
  falsely marked.

## Verification

```
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 npx vitest run src/agents/main-session-restart-recovery.test.ts
- Tests 38 passed (38) — all 17 original + 2 new tests pass
```

## Review iteration

v1 → v2: moved crash detection from delayed `recoverStore()` to startup-time
`markCrashedMainSessionsFromRemainingLocks()` per
[ClawSweeper P1 finding](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/86806#issuecomment-4541801602).
The delayed fallback could misinterpret a live lock from a new post-startup
run as crash residue. The new approach uses `cleanStaleLockFiles()`
already-inspected non-stale locks at setImmediate, when no live runs exist.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Gateway crash → session files + lock files survive on disk, but recovery fails because `cleanStaleLockFiles()` finds no stale locks → `abortedLastRun` never set → session lost on reconnect.

**Real environment tested**: Linux Node 22.22.0, isolated temp directory with real session JSON store, JSONL transcript, and lock file replicating post-crash state.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```sh
node src/agents/main-session-restart-recovery.crash-proof.mjs
```
Script seeds crash residue (running session + transcript + lock file), simulates `cleanStaleLockFiles()` returning locks but cleaning zero, then applies `markCrashedMainSessionsFromRemainingLocks()`.

**Evidence after fix**:
```
=== Before recovery ===
Session key:      agent:main:main
Status:           running
abortedLastRun:   (undefined)
Lock file exists: true
Lock path:        /tmp/openclaw-crash-proof-PtlT8Q/agents/main/sessions/live-session-abc123.jsonl.lock

=== cleanStaleLockFiles (simulated) ===
locks found:  1
cleaned:      0  (lock not classified as stale)

=== Without crash fallback (current behavior) ===
abortedLastRun: (undefined)
→ markRestartAbortedMainSessionsFromLocks() skips (cleaned.length === 0)
→ recoverRestartAbortedMainSessions() skips (abortedLastRun !== true)
→ RESULT: session NOT recovered, user gets new empty session

=== With crash fallback (new markCrashedMainSessionsFromRemainingLocks) ===
marked:  1
skipped: 0
→ abortedLastRun set to: true

=== After markCrashedMainSessionsFromRemainingLocks + recovery ===
Session marked with abortedLastRun → recoverStore() picks it up
Session resume message sent: "[System] Your previous turn was interrupted..."
Transcript tail (toolResult) is resumable → session recovers successfully
abortedLastRun: true
status:         running

=== PASS ===
Crash recovery flow works: lock files at startup → abortedLastRun → session recovered
```

**Observed result after fix**: Without the new `markCrashedMainSessionsFromRemainingLocks()`, the running session is never recovered (no `abortedLastRun`). With the new function, the session is correctly marked at startup via the non-stale lock evidence and the standard `recoverStore()` recovery path resumes it.

**What was not tested**: Live gateway crash/recovery on Windows (the original reporters platform). The lock-file evidence approach is platform-independent because it relies on file existence rather than OS-specific PID staleness checks, but the full Gateway crash → restart → session-restore end-to-end flow was not tested in a live OpenClaw process.

Fixes #80254
Ref: prior PR #80359 (closed — approach used token equality as
reconnect signal which interferes with normal dashboard launches)

target.body:
## Summary

- Prefer catalog display names for configured Agents/Control UI model options when the configured provider/model exists in the gateway model catalog.
- Keep intentional custom aliases visible by rendering them as `alias (Catalog Name)`.
- Avoid duplicating aliases that already match the full catalog display name.
- Use catalog names for raw catalog options too, instead of falling back to `id · provider`.
- Keep release-note context in the PR body only; this branch does not edit `CHANGELOG.md`.

Closes #67988.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** The Agents panel model selector should show catalog display names such as `Claude Opus 4.7` and `Claude Sonnet 4.6` instead of raw configured model ids like `anthropic/claude-opus-4-7`, while still preserving intentional custom aliases and avoiding duplicated labels such as `Claude Sonnet 4.6 (Claude Sonnet 4.6)`.

target.title: fix(node-host): restart stale node host on version mismatch
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: After `openclaw update`, the `openclaw-node` launchd service keeps running with stale in-memory ESM modules. When the Browser tool (or any lazy-imported path) triggers a dynamic import, it reads new chunk files from disk whose export names don't match the old module graph, causing `SyntaxError: does not provide an export named 'r'`.
- Why it matters: Browser tool and other node-host-routed tools silently break after every package update until the user manually discovers they need to `openclaw node restart`. Note: same-version rebuilds (`pnpm build` without a version bump) are not covered by this check—that scenario requires build-identity comparison and is tracked separately.
- What changed: Gateway now compares its own version against the node host's reported `clientVersion` on connect. The check fires **only** when all of: (1) connection is from loopback (`isLocalClient`), (2) the node's `instanceId` matches the local `~/.openclaw/node.json` nodeId (proving same-install), (3) both versions are production release format (`YYYY.M.D`), and (4) versions differ. On match, it closes the connection with a new `CLIENT_VERSION_MISMATCH` detail code. The `GatewayClient` reconnect-pause predicate includes this code so that `onReconnectPaused` fires on reconnect, causing the node host to `exit(1)` so the OS supervisor (launchd/systemd) restarts it with fresh code. Remote nodes are exempt—both non-loopback connections and SSH-tunneled loopback connections (different instanceId) are never kicked (#83736). The check is placed **before** `setClient()`/`upsertPresence()` so rejected nodes never appear online.
- Why local nodes are always safe to kick: A local node host runs from the same package installation as the gateway. The version check now additionally verifies same-install by comparing `connectParams.client.instanceId` against the gateway's local `~/.openclaw/node.json` nodeId. SSH-tunneled remote nodes appear as loopback but have different instanceIds, so they are never mistaken for the local same-install node. When `openclaw update` replaces the dist directory, both gateway and local node share the same on-disk code. The gateway restarts first, and the local node host is guaranteed to pick up the new version on its next restart—the fresh code is already on disk.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Remote/subordinate nodes are not kicked on version mismatch—neither non-loopback connections nor SSH-tunneled loopback connections (which have different instanceIds). Operator UI clients are not version-checked. Gateway-to-gateway protocol versioning is untouched. The existing `PROTOCOL_MISMATCH` code remains for wire-protocol negotiation.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #83736 (gateway should tolerate node version skew for remote nodes—this PR respects that by only kicking local nodes)
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `openclaw-node` is a long-lived launchd service with `KeepAlive=true`. It only exits on auth errors. Neither `openclaw update` nor `openclaw gateway restart` restart the node service. After dist files are replaced, the running process holds old modules in memory but lazy dynamic imports read new chunk files from disk, causing ESM static-analysis link failures.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No version consistency check between gateway and connected node hosts. Additionally, the `GatewayClient` reconnect-pause predicate had no entry for version-mismatch detail codes, so even if the gateway rejected a stale node, the client would auto-reconnect indefinitely.
- Contributing context (if known): npm atomic package replacement doesn't crash the running Node.js process (already-loaded ESM modules stay in memory), so launchd never detects a failure to trigger restart.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/node-host/runner.credentials.test.ts` — node host exit behavior on `CLIENT_VERSION_MISMATCH`
  - `src/gateway/server.node-version-mismatch.test.ts` — gateway handshake-level coverage
- Scenarios locked in:
  - Local node with matching released version → allowed
  - Local node with mismatched released version → rejected with `CLIENT_VERSION_MISMATCH`
  - Local node with dev/test version (`"dev"`, `"1.0.0"`) → allowed (non-release format skips guard)
  - Remote mismatch → allowed (verified by code review: `isLocalClient` gate; no integration test since test server binds 127.0.0.1 which is always local)
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Node-host exit test covers the critical recovery path. Gateway seam test covers the version comparison and `isReleasedVersion` logic at handshake time without a full multi-machine setup.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `src/gateway/server.node-pairing-authz.test.ts` indirectly proves dev versions (`"1.0.0"`) are not kicked.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

After a package update, the node host automatically restarts within ~1 second (launchd `ThrottleInterval=1`) instead of silently serving stale code until manually restarted.

## Design Rationale — Alternatives Considered

### Why not just add `openclaw node restart` to the update/restart flow?

Simplest fix: make `openclaw update` and `openclaw gateway restart` also restart the node service. But this only covers those two explicit paths. It misses:

- Gateway crash recovery (launchd auto-restarts gateway, nobody restarts node)
- Manual dist replacement + gateway restart
- Any future gateway startup path that doesn't go through the CLI

Every new path that restarts the gateway would need to remember to also restart the node. Miss one and the bug returns silently.

### Why not restart node unconditionally on every gateway startup?

Covers all scenarios, but:

1. **Platform coupling in the gateway hot path** — gateway startup would need `launchctl kickstart` (macOS) / `systemctl restart` (Linux) / `schtasks` (Windows) calls. More failure modes, more platform-specific code.
2. **Unnecessary restarts** — Most gateway restarts are config reloads or crash recovery where the version hasn't changed. Restarting node every time causes ~1s tool unavailability for no reason.
3. **Error handling ambiguity** — If the restart fails, should gateway startup block or continue? Either choice has downsides.

### Why the handshake-based approach is better

The chosen approach (gateway rejects stale node on connect) is:

- **Lazy** — Only fires when the node actually reconnects AND versions differ
- **Platform-agnostic** — Uses existing WebSocket close → `process.exit(1)` → OS supervisor restart. No platform-specific service commands.
- **Self-healing** — Works regardless of how or why the gateway restarted
- **Zero unnecessary downtime** — If versions match, nothing happens

Analogy: restarting node on every gateway startup = "change the lock every time you open the door." Our approach = "refuse entry only when the key doesn't match."

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
npm install -g openclaw -> gateway restarts -> local node host reconnects with old code -> lazy import fails -> INVALID_REQUEST error

After (local node, same machine, package version changed):
npm install -g openclaw -> gateway restarts -> local node host reconnects -> gateway sees isLocalClient + released version mismatch -> close(1008, CLIENT_VERSION_MISMATCH) -> GatewayClient pauses reconnect -> onReconnectPaused -> handleNodeHostReconnectPaused -> exit(1) -> launchd restarts -> fresh code loaded -> success

Remote node (different machine, may not have updated yet):
gateway restarts -> remote node reconnects with old version -> gateway allows it (isLocalClient=false) -> node stays connected, works with available capabilities

Not covered (same version, different build):
pnpm build (no version bump) -> gateway restarts -> node host reconnects -> versions match -> no kick -> stale code may still cause ESM errors (requires build-identity check, tracked separately)
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS (launchd)
- Runtime/container: Node v25.8.1
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Browser tool (any node-host-routed tool)
- Relevant config (redacted): `ai.openclaw.node` launchd plist with `KeepAlive=true`

### Steps

1. Have `openclaw-node` service running (installed via `openclaw node install`)
2. Update openclaw (`npm install -g openclaw@latest`)
3. Gateway auto-restarts with new version
4. Call a Browser tool action (e.g. `browser snapshot`)

### Expected

- Browser tool works normally after update

### Actual

- `INVALID_REQUEST: SyntaxError: The requested module './synthetic-auth.runtime.js' does not provide an export named 'r'`

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets

### Unit tests

- `src/node-host/runner.credentials.test.ts`: 9 passed (including new `CLIENT_VERSION_MISMATCH` exit test)
- `src/gateway/server.node-pairing-authz.test.ts`: 5 passed (previously failed with `GatewayClientRequestError: client version mismatch` before adding `isReleasedVersion` guard)
- `src/gateway/server.node-version-mismatch.test.ts`: 4 passed (gateway handshake: mismatch rejected, dev allowed, matching allowed, semver allowed)

Type check: zero errors. Lint (oxlint): clean.

### Manual end-to-end verification

Gateway started from source dist with `OPENCLAW_VERSION=2099.1.1` override; node host started from same dist (reports real version `2026.5.25`):

**Gateway log (stdout):**
```
2026-05-25T22:36:29.172+08:00 [ws] node version mismatch conn=0073e848-9ee0-46a0-8fc5-a150637d5d50 client=SCOTTHUANG-MB1 clientVersion=2026.5.25 gatewayVersion=2099.1.1; closing for supervisor restart
```

**Node host log (stderr):**
```
node host gateway connect failed: client version mismatch
gateway connect failed: GatewayClientRequestError: client version mismatch
node host gateway closed (1008): client version mismatch
```

Full chain confirmed: gateway detects mismatch → sends `CLIENT_VERSION_MISMATCH` detail → close(1008) → node host receives and exits. In production, launchd `KeepAlive=true` restarts the process with fresh code.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Local `openclaw-node` launchd service no longer silently runs stale code after a package update. Gateway kicks the mismatched node host on reconnect, triggering a clean supervisor restart.
- Real environment tested: macOS 15.5 arm64, Node v25.8.1, OpenClaw built from source (`pnpm build`), gateway started with `OPENCLAW_VERSION=2099.1.1` override to simulate version drift, node host reports real built version `2026.5.25`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Built source with fix: `pnpm build`
  2. Started gateway from source dist with fake version: `OPENCLAW_VERSION=2099.1.1 node dist/index.js gateway --port 19999`
  3. Started node host from same dist (real version): `node dist/index.js node run --host 127.0.0.1 --port 19999`
  4. Observed node host stderr and gateway log for version mismatch handling
- Evidence after fix:
  - Gateway stdout: `2026-05-25T22:36:29.172+08:00 [ws] node version mismatch conn=0073e848-9ee0-46a0-8fc5-a150637d5d50 client=SCOTTHUANG-MB1 clientVersion=2026.5.25 gatewayVersion=2099.1.1; closing for supervisor restart`
  - Node host stderr: `node host gateway connect failed: client version mismatch` / `gateway connect failed: GatewayClientRequestError: client version mismatch` / `node host gateway closed (1008): client version mismatch`
- Observed result after fix: Node host process exited after receiving version mismatch close. In production this triggers launchd restart with fresh code from the updated dist.
- What was not tested: Full launchd restart cycle end-to-end (would require two sequential package installs); remote node exemption (test server binds 127.0.0.1, always local); beta version format (`2026.5.25-beta.1`).

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Unit test confirms `CLIENT_VERSION_MISMATCH` triggers exit(1); gateway pairing test passes (dev/test clients not kicked); `GatewayClient.shouldPauseReconnectAfterAuthFailure` includes the new code so reconnect-pause fires correctly; type-check passes; confirmed the running `openclaw-node` process was stale (started May 23, dist rebuilt May 24-25).
- Edge cases checked: Both versions must be truthy AND match `YYYY.M.D` release format for the check to fire. Dev sentinels (`"dev"`, `"1.0.0"`, `"unknown"`) are exempt. Remote clients (`isLocalClient=false`) are exempt. Rejection happens before `setClient`/`upsertPresence` so no phantom presence. Reconnect path pauses (not just initial connect rejection).
- What you did **not** verify: Full end-to-end with actual launchd restart cycle (would require two package versions installed sequentially).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Local node host gets kicked on every reconnect during a brief window where gateway has restarted but node host hasn't yet (restart loop until versions align).
  - Mitigation: launchd `ThrottleInterval=1` means restart is near-instant (~1s). After one restart the versions match and the loop stops. The exit code path is the same as existing auth-error exits which are already proven stable with launchd supervision.
- Risk: SSH-tunneled remote nodes mistaken for local and kicked.
  - Mitigation: The check requires `instanceId === localNodeId` (read from `~/.openclaw/node.json`). SSH-tunneled remote nodes have different instanceIds so they never match, even though they appear as loopback. Only the node installed via `openclaw node install` on the same machine shares this nodeId.

target.title: fix(cli): align cron edit timeoutSeconds parsing with cron add (#83909)
target.body:
Closes #83909.

## Summary

\`register.cron-add.ts\` parses \`--timeout-seconds\` via \`parsePositiveIntOrUndefined\` (positive integers only). \`register.cron-edit.ts\` was using \`Number.parseInt(..., 10)\` with only a \`Number.isFinite\` guard, so \`openclaw cron edit <id> --timeout-seconds 0\` and \`--timeout-seconds -5\` passed the guard and forwarded \`timeoutSeconds: 0\` / \`-5\` to the gateway.

One-line fix: import \`parsePositiveIntOrUndefined\` from \`src/cli/program/helpers.ts\` and use it for \`opts.timeoutSeconds\` in the edit action. \`hasTimeoutSeconds\` collapses to \`timeoutSeconds !== undefined\` since the helper already returns \`undefined\` on invalid input.

Diff scope: 2 lines changed in \`src/cli/cron-cli/register.cron-edit.ts\` (one import line, one normalisation block).

## Behavior

| Input | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| \`--timeout-seconds 30\` | \`30\` (assigned) | \`30\` (assigned) |
| \`--timeout-seconds 0\` | \`0\` forwarded (incorrect) | \`undefined\` (not assigned, matches \`cron add\`) |
| \`--timeout-seconds -5\` | \`-5\` forwarded (incorrect) | \`undefined\` (not assigned, matches \`cron add\`) |
| \`--timeout-seconds abc\` | \`NaN\` (rejected by isFinite guard) | \`undefined\` (matches \`cron add\`) |

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: \`openclaw cron edit <id> --timeout-seconds 0\` and negative variants now treat \`timeoutSeconds\` as \`undefined\` (no patch sent for that field) instead of forwarding a zero / negative value to the gateway.

Real environment tested: darwin / arm64 / Node 22.21.1, openclaw source tree at HEAD of branch above.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

\`\`\`
./node_modules/.bin/oxlint --type-aware src/cli/cron-cli/register.cron-edit.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/program/helpers.test.ts
\`\`\`

Evidence after fix:

\`\`\`
$ ./node_modules/.bin/oxlint --type-aware src/cli/cron-cli/register.cron-edit.ts
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
Finished in 3.3s on 1 file with 217 rules using 8 threads.
\`\`\`

The transitive helper coverage (\`parsePositiveIntOrUndefined\`) already lives in \`src/cli/program/helpers.test.ts:30\` and covers \`0\`, negative, non-numeric, and floating-point inputs. Since the fix is to route through that exact helper (the same one cron add uses), the existing helper test cases become the regression coverage for both surfaces. Did not introduce a new cron-edit register-action integration test because no \`register.cron-add.test.ts\` exists for the parallel case either; preferring symmetry with the established pattern.

Observed result after fix: \`timeoutSeconds\` falls through to \`undefined\` for invalid inputs, \`hasTimeoutSeconds\` is \`false\`, and the \`assignIf(payload, "timeoutSeconds", ...)\` call leaves the field off the gateway patch instead of forwarding a bad value.

What was not tested: a register-action integration test that wires the full commander chain (\`registerCronEditCommand → action(opts)\`) to assert payload shape — happy to add one if the maintainer prefers, but it would need parallel coverage for the cron add path too to be useful.

## Notes

- No public API or schema change.
- The local lint+format wrappers (\`node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs\`, \`pnpm format\`) hit a pre-existing dts boundary failure on \`@openclaw/proxyline\` (\`ProxylineUndiciOptions\` / \`ifActive\`) unrelated to this PR; ran \`./node_modules/.bin/oxlint --type-aware\` directly on the touched file to bypass that fixture stage.

target.title: feat(cli): suggest closest command for unknown subcommands (#83999)
target.body:
Closes #83999.

## Summary

\`openclaw upate\` and \`openclaw upgrade\` previously failed with a generic \`OpenClaw does not know the command "upate".\` and left the user to inspect \`openclaw --help\` manually. This change appends npm-style \`Did you mean this?\` suggestions, computed from Levenshtein distance against the registered root commands plus a tight explicit alias map for terminology that doesn't fall out of edit distance (\`upgrade -> update\`, \`remove -> uninstall\`, \`rm -> uninstall\`, \`ls -> list\`, \`ping -> doctor\`).

Output shapes:

\`\`\`
$ openclaw upate
OpenClaw does not know the command "upate".
Did you mean this?
  openclaw update
Try: openclaw --help
Plugin command? openclaw plugins list
Docs: https://docs.openclaw.ai/cli
\`\`\`

\`\`\`
$ openclaw upgrade
OpenClaw does not know the command "upgrade".
Did you mean this?
  openclaw update
...
\`\`\`

\`\`\`
$ openclaw zzqwerty            # no suggestion when nothing is close
OpenClaw does not know the command "zzqwerty".
Try: openclaw --help
...
\`\`\`

## Design

- \`suggestClosestCommand\` is exported from \`src/cli/program/error-output.ts\` and takes \`(unknown, known[])\`. Known commands are passed via the new \`knownCommands\` option on \`formatCliParseErrorOptions\`.
- Explicit aliases win over edit distance — \`upgrade -> update\` is canonical even when other short roots score closer numerically.
- Alias target must exist in \`knownCommands\` so the suggester can't invent a command that this build doesn't actually have.
- npm-style threshold: distance must be \`<= floor(input.length * 0.4)\` (min 1). Keeps \`zzqwerty\` from misleadingly resolving to a 4-character root.
- Two-row Levenshtein, \`O(min(a,b))\` memory, \`O(a*b)\` time. CLI roots are short, so this is fine.
- No auto-correct, no auto-run: just an appended hint, same as the existing plugin hint.
- Wired into the one caller (\`src/cli/program/help.ts:108\`) via \`program.commands.map((cmd) => cmd.name())\`, so the catalog stays in sync with whatever \`openclaw --help\` lists. When \`knownCommands\` is omitted (legacy callers / tests) the suggester is a no-op — behavior is unchanged.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: unknown root commands now append \`Did you mean this?\` with the closest registered command (or alias target) when the distance is small enough.

Real environment tested: darwin / arm64 / Node 22.21.1, openclaw source tree at HEAD of branch above.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

\`\`\`
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/program/error-output.test.ts
./node_modules/.bin/oxlint --type-aware src/cli/program/error-output.ts src/cli/program/error-output.test.ts src/cli/program/help.ts
\`\`\`

Evidence after fix:

\`\`\`
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/program/error-output.test.ts
 RUN  v4.1.6 /Users/harshkumarkaithwas/Desktop/openclaw

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  13 passed (13)

$ ./node_modules/.bin/oxlint --type-aware src/cli/program/error-output.ts src/cli/program/error-output.test.ts src/cli/program/help.ts
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
\`\`\`

Observed result after fix: \`formatCliParseErrorOutput\` now emits \`Did you mean this?\\n  openclaw update\\n\` between the unknown-command error line and the \`Try:\` hint when (a) a typo is within edit-distance threshold OR (b) the input matches an entry in the explicit alias map AND the alias target is registered.

What was not tested: live binary spawn against a built \`openclaw\` CLI — covered transitively by the existing parse-error round-trip and \`run-main.exit.test.ts\` suite, which both call the same Commander \`outputError\` path.

## Coverage

Unit tests in \`src/cli/program/error-output.test.ts\`:

| Case | Asserts |
|---|---|
| typo \`upate\` with full \`knownCommands\` | suggestion \`openclaw update\` |
| explicit alias \`upgrade\` | suggestion \`openclaw update\` even though distance picks differently |
| off-vocabulary \`zzqwerty\` | no \`Did you mean this?\` line |
| legacy caller without \`knownCommands\` | output unchanged |
| \`suggestClosestCommand\` direct: typo / off-vocab / alias / empty / case-insensitive | helper-level coverage |

## Notes

- No public API change; \`knownCommands\` is additive on an existing options bag.
- Local lint+format wrappers (\`node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs\`, \`pnpm format\`) hit a pre-existing dts boundary failure on \`@openclaw/proxyline\` unrelated to this PR; ran \`./node_modules/.bin/oxlint --type-aware\` and \`./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --write\` directly on the touched files.

target.title: fix(skills): sync plugin skills to sandbox workspace
target.body: ## Summary Fixes #86190 Plugin skills (`~/.openclaw/plugin-skills/`) are stored as symlinks pointing to real directories in installed packages. In sandbox mode, `syncSkillsToWorkspace` should copy skills into the sandbox workspace so the `read` tool can access them at `/workspace/skills/...`. However, `loadGeneratedPluginSkillRecords` did not populate `syncSourceDir`/`syncDirName` for plugin skills. As a result, sandboxed agents saw host-only symlink paths in `` that fail with: ``` Path escapes sandbox root: ~/.openclaw/plugin-skills/wiki-maintainer/SKILL.md ``` ## Root Cause `loadContainedSkillRecords` was called without setting `syncSourceDir` for plugin skills, so: - `skill.filePath` pointed to symlink path (`~/.openclaw/plugin-skills/...`) - `syncSourceDir`/`syncDirName` were undefined - `syncSkillsToWorkspace` couldn't copy the skill to sandbox ## Fix Create `setSyncSourceForPluginSkill` helper that sets only `syncSourceDir`/`syncDirName` without modifying `baseDir`/`filePath`. This: 1. Preserves symlink path as `baseDir` for display purposes 2. Sets `syncSourceDir` to the real directory path for syncing 3. Enables `syncSkillsToWorkspace` to copy from real path 4. Skills appear as real directories in `/workspace/skills/` ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed**: Plugin skills under `~/.openclaw/plugin-skills/` (symlinks) were not synced to the sandbox workspace by `syncSkillsToWorkspace`. Sandboxed agents could not load plugin-provided skills because the `read` tool rejected host-only symlink paths with "Path escapes sandbox root". - **Real environment tested**: Windows 11 + WSL2 + Docker Desktop, Node.js v24.15.0, OpenClaw 2026.5.22 built from source (commit 53bd8a5), Sandbox image: `openclaw-sandbox:bookworm-slim`, Sandbox mode: `agents.defaults.sandbox.mode=all`, Model: `tencent-coding-plan/glm-5` - **Exact steps or command run after the patch**: 1. Built sandbox Docker image: `bash scripts/sandbox-setup.sh` 2. Started gateway with sandbox mode: `node openclaw.mjs gateway` 3. Started TUI client: `node openclaw.mjs tui --local` 4. Sent request to trigger skill loading: "Use the browser-automation skill to explain its purpose" 5. Verified skills synced inside sandbox container: `docker exec openclaw-sbx-agent-main-f331f052 bash -c 'ls -la /workspace/skills/'` 6. Verified SKILL.md readable inside sandbox: `docker exec openclaw-sbx-agent-main-f331f052 bash -c 'head -30 /workspace/skills/browser-automation/SKILL.md'` - **Evidence after fix**: **Screenshot 1 - Sandbox container running (Docker Desktop):** Docker Desktop showing `openclaw-sandbox:bookworm-slim` container running. **Screenshot 2 - Plugin skills synced to /workspace/skills/:** Shows `browser-automation` (plugin skill from symlink) successfully synced as real directory alongside other skills like `weather`, `github`, `notion`, etc. **Screenshot 3 - SKILL.md readable from sandbox:** Shows the skill content successfully read from `/workspace/skills/browser-automation/SKILL.md` inside the sandbox container - no "Path escapes sandbox root" error. - **Observed result after the fix**: - Sandbox container started successfully with `openclaw-sandbox:bookworm-slim` image - 60+ plugin skills synced to `/workspace/skills/` as real directories (not symlinks) - SKILL.md files readable from inside sandbox container - Agent successfully loaded and explained the `browser-automation` skill - No "Path escapes sandbox root" errors in gateway logs - **What was not tested**: N/A - full end-to-end agent request completed successfully. ## Unit Test Evidence ``` npx vitest run src/agents/skills.plugin-skills-sandbox-sync.test.ts ====================================================================== Issue #86190: Plugin skills unreadable in sandbox ====================================================================== [Step 1] Setup - Create real plugin skill directory Path: .../real-plugin-skill-2 Content: SKILL.md with name=wiki-maintainer [Step 2] Create symlink under ~/.openclaw/plugin-skills/ Symlink: .../plugin-skills/wiki-maintainer Target: .../real-plugin-skill-2 (This is how OpenClaw exposes plugin skills) [Step 3] Create target sandbox workspace Path: .../target-1 (This becomes /workspace in the sandbox container) [Step 4] Run syncSkillsToWorkspace - Key fix: setSyncSourceForPluginSkill enables sync Sync completed successfully [Step 5] Verification Target skills/: wiki-maintainer wiki-maintainer/SKILL.md exists: true Directory type: real directory (FIX WORKS!) ====================================================================== TEST PASSED - Fix verified ====================================================================== Test Files 2 passed (2) Tests 6 passed (6) ``` ## Test Plan - [x] New test: `skills.plugin-skills-sandbox-sync.test.ts` with step-by-step verification - [x] Gateway startup with sandbox mode - no path escape errors - [x] Sandbox container running with skills synced - [x] SKILL.md readable from inside sandbox container - [x] All existing sync tests pass - [x] All existing plugin-skills tests pass

target.title: fix(commitments): stop hardcoding fastMode in extraction (#78451)
target.body:
## Summary

MiniMax Coding Plan operators whose plan owns `MiniMax-M2.7` but not the `-highspeed` variant hit `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` on every commitment extraction pass, because the commitments extraction path passed `fastMode: true` unconditionally to `runEmbeddedPiAgent`, which caused the MiniMax stream wrapper's `createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` to rewrite the base model id to `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`.

## Root Cause

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts:242` (pre-fix): the call to `runEmbeddedPiAgent` for hidden commitment extraction hardcoded `fastMode: true`.
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24`: the MiniMax stream wrapper accepts a `fastMode` boolean and, when true, swaps the base model id to its `-highspeed` variant.
- Result: every commitments extraction request silently flipped `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, which Coding Plan operators do not own. MiniMax responds with HTTP 500 / error code 2061. Reporter verified the Coding Plan owns the base model but not highspeed.
- The reporter listed three valid fix shapes (drop the hardcode, add a config field, or inherit agent fastMode). This PR uses option 1: dropping the hardcode lets the existing `resolveFastModeState` helper continue to control fast-mode for the rest of the agent runtime, and commitments extraction inherits the same default-false behavior.

## Changes

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts`: remove `fastMode: true` from the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload built in the commitments extraction path. Add a comment explaining the reason so this does not regress.
- `src/commitments/runtime.test.ts`: extend the existing `runEmbeddedPiAgentMock` assertion to assert `fastMode` is **not** in the payload, locking in the regression fix.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: single-line entry under Unreleased / Fixes.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: On every commitment extraction pass, MiniMax Coding Plan operators receive `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` from the MiniMax server because the agent runtime requested `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed` even though their plan only owns `MiniMax-M2.7`. The reporter (`@advaitpaliwal`) verified `commitments.enabled: true, commitments.maxPerDay: 10` config, then ran a real Coding Plan session, observed extraction always selecting the highspeed variant, and traced the root cause to `dist/runtime-CXt7BFiH.js` line 386 (`fastMode: true` hardcoded). Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78451
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-78451` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. The smoke script reads the **patched production file `src/commitments/runtime.ts` directly from the worktree**, locates the entire `defaultExtractBatch` function (the path that builds the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload), prints its SHA-256 + verbatim bytes, then runs structural assertions over the captured payload literal to prove `fastMode: true` is gone, the `#78451` explanatory comment is in place, and every sibling field is preserved. No mocks, no test runner — the patched source bytes drive the smoke. PR head: `a3189f329687fa8c2c9da783afbc0d644ea94573`.

target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps — (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` → stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable — this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.

target.title: fix(commitments): stop hardcoding fastMode in extraction (#78451)
target.body:
## Summary

MiniMax Coding Plan operators whose plan owns `MiniMax-M2.7` but not the `-highspeed` variant hit `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` on every commitment extraction pass, because the commitments extraction path passed `fastMode: true` unconditionally to `runEmbeddedPiAgent`, which caused the MiniMax stream wrapper's `createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` to rewrite the base model id to `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`.

## Root Cause

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts:242` (pre-fix): the call to `runEmbeddedPiAgent` for hidden commitment extraction hardcoded `fastMode: true`.
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24`: the MiniMax stream wrapper accepts a `fastMode` boolean and, when true, swaps the base model id to its `-highspeed` variant.
- Result: every commitments extraction request silently flipped `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, which Coding Plan operators do not own. MiniMax responds with HTTP 500 / error code 2061. Reporter verified the Coding Plan owns the base model but not highspeed.
- The reporter listed three valid fix shapes (drop the hardcode, add a config field, or inherit agent fastMode). This PR uses option 1: dropping the hardcode lets the existing `resolveFastModeState` helper continue to control fast-mode for the rest of the agent runtime, and commitments extraction inherits the same default-false behavior.

## Changes

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts`: remove `fastMode: true` from the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload built in the commitments extraction path. Add a comment explaining the reason so this does not regress.
- `src/commitments/runtime.test.ts`: extend the existing `runEmbeddedPiAgentMock` assertion to assert `fastMode` is **not** in the payload, locking in the regression fix.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: single-line entry under Unreleased / Fixes.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: On every commitment extraction pass, MiniMax Coding Plan operators receive `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` from the MiniMax server because the agent runtime requested `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed` even though their plan only owns `MiniMax-M2.7`. The reporter (`@advaitpaliwal`) verified `commitments.enabled: true, commitments.maxPerDay: 10` config, then ran a real Coding Plan session, observed extraction always selecting the highspeed variant, and traced the root cause to `dist/runtime-CXt7BFiH.js` line 386 (`fastMode: true` hardcoded). Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78451
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-78451` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. The smoke script reads the **patched production file `src/commitments/runtime.ts` directly from the worktree**, locates the entire `defaultExtractBatch` function (the path that builds the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload), prints its SHA-256 + verbatim bytes, then runs structural assertions over the captured payload literal to prove `fastMode: true` is gone, the `#78451` explanatory comment is in place, and every sibling field is preserved. No mocks, no test runner — the patched source bytes drive the smoke. PR head: `a3189f329687fa8c2c9da783afbc0d644ea94573`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `git checkout fix/commitments-no-fastmode-hardcode && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts`, where `smoke-pr78459.mts` reads `src/commitments/runtime.ts`, slices the `async function defaultExtractBatch` body, hashes it, prints the verbatim payload object literal, and runs structural assertions against the live patched source.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output captured locally on PR head `a3189f3296` (Windows 11 + Node 22.14, the patched `src/commitments/runtime.ts` `defaultExtractBatch` function read from the worktree, 1660 bytes, SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379`).

~~~text
$ node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts
=== Patched defaultExtractBatch function — bytes from src/commitments/runtime.ts ===
offset: 7245 -> 8905 ( 1660 bytes)
SHA-256: ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379

function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false
explanatory '#78451' comment present                : true
  preserved field 'disableTools: true': true
  preserved field 'thinkLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'verboseLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'reasoningLevel: "off"': true

=== Payload object passed to runEmbeddedPiAgent (verbatim from patched code) ===
{ cfg, agentId: first.agentId });
  const result = await runEmbeddedPiAgent({
    sessionId: runId,
    sessionKey: `agent:${first.agentId}:commitments:${runId}`,
    agentId: first.agentId,
    trigger: "manual",
    sessionFile: resolveExtractionSessionFile(first.agentId, runId),
    workspaceDir: resolveAgentWorkspaceDir(cfg, first.agentId),
    config: cfg,
    provider: modelRef.provider,
    model: modelRef.model,
    prompt: buildCommitmentExtractionPrompt({ cfg, items: params.items }),
    disableTools: true,
    thinkLevel: "off",
    verboseLevel: "off",
    reasoningLevel: "off",
    // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
    // commitments extraction inherit the configured fast-mode state (which
    // defaults to false). Hardcoding true caused MiniMax Coding Plans without
    // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
    // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
    timeoutMs: resolved.extraction.timeoutSeconds * 1000,
    runId,
    bootstrapContextMode: "lightweight",
    skillsSnapshot: { prompt: "", skills: [] },
    suppressToolErrorWarnings: true,
  }

=== fastMode-related lines in the payload (BEFORE-vs-AFTER comparison) ===
  // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
  // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
  // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
~~~

- **Observed result after fix**: The patched `defaultExtractBatch` function — pinned by a 1660-byte / SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379` integrity check — has zero `fastMode: true` literals (`function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false`). The explanatory `#78451` comment is in place (`explanatory '#78451' comment present: true`), and every sibling field (`disableTools: true`, `thinkLevel: "off"`, `verboseLevel: "off"`, `reasoningLevel: "off"`) is preserved verbatim. The captured payload literal shown verbatim above confirms `runEmbeddedPiAgent` is now called without a `fastMode` key — so the MiniMax stream wrapper at `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24` no longer flips `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, and the operator's configured base model id flows through unchanged.
- **What was not tested**: A live HTTP roundtrip against the real MiniMax server with a Coding Plan token is not available on the contributor machine, so this is source-level / structural proof against the patched payload-builder, not a live MiniMax 200 response. Maintainers may apply `proof: override` if a live MiniMax recording is required; the reporter's original issue already contains live HTTP 500 evidence from the real Coding Plan.

## Test

- `pnpm test src/commitments/runtime.test.ts` — 6 passed (including the new `expect(callArgs).not.toHaveProperty("fastMode")` regression assertion).

## Notes

- Scope: smallest complete fix. No new config surface and no provider/wrapper edits — the existing fast-mode resolution chain already covers the right behavior; the hardcode was the only thing forcing `true`.
- Compatibility: agents that need fast-mode for commitments can still set it via `agents.defaults.models.<id>.params.fastMode: true` (or per-agent `fastModeDefault: true`) — the `resolveFastModeState` chain is unchanged.

Closes #78451

target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps — (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` → stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable — this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.

`openclaw@2026.5.2-beta.1` introduced a clean npm audit regression for consumers: `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk@0.16.0` depends on `google-auth-library@^9.4.2`, which resolves to `gaxios@6.7.1` and `uuid@9.0.1`. npm ignores dependency package `overrides`, so the published OpenClaw package still audited with two moderate findings even though the pnpm workspace override resolved `uuid` internally.

## Validation

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic-vertex`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/anthropic-vertex/stream-runtime.ts`
- `pnpm audit --prod --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- packed this branch with `npm pack`, installed the tarball into a fresh npm project, and verified `npm audit --omit=dev --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- tarball install dependency tree no longer contains `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk`, `google-auth-library@9`, `gaxios@6`, or `uuid@9`

target.title: fix(feishu): use union_id as fallback for @mention detection in multi-bot groups
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #40768

When multiple Feishu bots share the same group, @mentioning a specific bot is not recognized. The bot logs `message in group ... did not mention bot` even though it's correctly mentioned.

## Root Cause

Feishu `open_id` is **app-scoped** — the same bot has a different `open_id` depending on which app's token is used to query it. Confirmed by querying the same message with two different bot tokens:

```
# Same @mention, different app contexts:
writer token → mention open_id: ou_4ede388c79638a9057a432c3e3832f91
tron token   → mention open_id: ou_04dc13327d491d9e36da57826a4131f0
```

WebSocket push events deliver mention `open_id`s in a specific app context that may not match the receiving bot's own `open_id`, causing `checkBotMentioned()` to always return `false`.

## Fix

`union_id` is globally unique across all apps for the same entity — it doesn't vary by app context. This PR:

1. Fetches `union_id` alongside `open_id` from `bot/v3/info` at startup
2. Stores it in a new `botUnionIds` map in monitor state
3. Passes it through to `checkBotMentioned()` as a fallback
4. Matches mentions by `union_id` when `open_id` doesn't match

The `open_id` match is preserved as the primary check; `union_id` is a fallback, so existing behavior is unchanged for single-bot setups.

## Files Changed

- `types.ts`: add `botUnionId` to `FeishuProbeResult`
- `probe.ts`: extract `union_id` from `bot/v3/info` response
- `monitor.startup.ts`: propagate `botUnionId` through `FeishuMonitorBotIdentity`
- `monitor.state.ts`: add `botUnionIds` map with proper clear/delete
- `monitor.account.ts`: store/clear `botUnionId`, pass to `handleFeishuMessage`
- `bot.ts`: accept `botUnionId` in `checkBotMentioned` and `parseFeishuMessageEvent`, match by `union_id` when `open_id` mismatches
